rulings.Case Title: GURSON S. DANG VS. SEAN M. CHANDLER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 26, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT SEAN CHANDLER MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, Or Alternatively, Summary Adjudication. Defendant Sean M. Chandler's motion for summary judgment is granted. Plaintiff's second amended complaint consists of 221 pages of largely unintelligible allegations and evidentiary material purportedly giving rise to 18 different causes of action against Mr. Chandler, who owns a unit in a four-unit building in which plaintiff Gurson S. Dang (who is representing himself in pro per) owns a second unit. Despite prior orders granting plaintiff leave to amend, the complaint still fails to allege facts sufficient to state any cause of action and is uncertain (ambiguous and unintelligible). The motion is granted on that ground alone. (See Stolz v. Wong Communications Limited Partnership (1994) 25 Cal.App.4th 1811, 1817 [the moving party "'need not . . . respond to assertions which are unintelligible or make out no recognizable legal claim,'" and court may treat a motion for summary judgment as a motion for judgment on the pleadings] [affirming summary judgment where complaint failed to state a cause of action.) Further, plaintiff does not contend in his opposition to the motion that there are any disputed issues of material fact, nor does he present any argument regarding the facts or the law. Rather, he opposes the motion on the sole ground that defendants did not submit a certified copy of his deposition transcript, but instead submitted an uncertified rough draft of that transcript. Plaintiff's 59-page declaration (with exhibits) in opposition to the motion relates almost entirely to that issue. However, Plaintiff's contention is false: the deposition transcript included in defendant's appendix of evidence as Exhibit II is a certified copy of that transcript dated June 13, 2019. Finally, plaintiff's separate statement does not comply with Code Civ. Proc. § 437c(b)(3) or Cal. R. Ct. 3.1350(f) in that it fails to refer to evidence supporting his position that facts are disputed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that he will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TRENA NELSON VS. DENTRUST DENTAL INTERNATIONAL, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Mental Examination Of Plaintiff Trena Nelson And, If Necessary, Augment Expert Witness List
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday September 26, 2019 Line 1 - DEFENDANT DENTRUST DENTAL INTERNATIONAL, INC.'s Motion To Compel Independent Mental Examination Of Plaintiff Trena Nelson And, If Necessary, Augment Expert Witness List. Pro Tem Judge Aaron Minnis, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Denied. The parties disclosed experts pursuant to CCP 2034.260. Neither party designated a retained expert. Plaintiff named two treating health care providers as non-retained experts: Dr. Armijo, who is a family physician; and Mr. Tock, a licensed social worker. Defendant cannot use a supplemental list to add Dr. Greene, a forensic psychiatrist, as a retained expert on new subjects not raised by Plaintiff's designation. See CCP 2034.280(a). Defendant also does not meet the statutory requirements for amending its expert witness list to add Dr. Greene. See CCP 2034.620(c). Defendant chose not to disclose an expert, or to seek a mental exam before now, despite having notice of Plaintiff's claim for mental and emotional distress from the pleadings and other discovery. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to aaron@minnisandsmallets.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANNE-MARIE WEBER VS. DAVID HESS NULL MD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Deem Requests For Admission, Set One, Admitted; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 26, 2019, Line 4, Motion To Deem Requests For Admission, Set One, Admitted; Request For Sanctions. The report and recommendation of the judge pro tem is rejected in part and adopted in part. Defendant's motion to deem requests for admission, set one, admitted is denied. Plaintiff served, before the hearing on the motion, a proposed response to the requests for admission (including a verification) that is in substantial compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 2033.220. (Code Civ. Proc. § 2033.280(c); see St. Mary v. Superior Court (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 762, 776 ["a responding party's service, prior to the hearing on the 'deemed admitted' motion, of substantially compliant responses, will defeat a propounding party's attempt under section 2033.280 to have the RFAs deemed admitted"].) If defendant believes that the responses to RFAs and accompanying responses to form interrogatories are deficient in some respect, it may make a motion to compel further responses. (Id.) Because plaintiff's failure to serve a timely response to the requests for admission necessitated defendant's motion, plaintiff is ordered to pay defendant $1,267.50 in sanctions. (Code Civ. Proc. § 2033.280(c).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: SHUI YING CHAN VS. KRISTIN DANETTE NELAND ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 26, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT KRISTIN NELAND, JOSH NELAND MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. (Part 1 of 2). Defendants' motion to strike portions of plaintiff's complaint is granted. Plaintiff seeks punitive damages, alleging that defendant "knowingly failed to stop at a stop sign and struck plaintiff, a pedestrian in a crosswalk, causing severe injuries." Plaintiff also alleges that defendant has multiple moving violations. Those allegations are insufficient to state a cognizable basis for an award of punitive damages. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code § 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) The allegations in the complaint that defendant ran a stop sign and had a history of moving violations do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-10 [trial court properly granted motion for summary adjudication of punitive damages where no reasonable juror could find that snowboarder's conduct was despicable; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her].) In Lackner, the court discussed with approval an out-of-state decision in which a court held that a driver who was speeding and was not paying attention to his driving, resulting in his rear-ending plaintiff's car while it was stopped at a four-way intersection, was not subject to an award of punitive damages. (Id. at 1212-13, discussing Mayfield v. Johnson (Miss. 1967) 202 So.2d 630.) The same result follows here. (See also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].) (Continued to Part 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SHUI YING CHAN VS. KRISTIN DANETTE NELAND ET AL

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, September 26, 2019, Line 6, Defendant Kristin Neland, Josh Neland Motion To Strike Complaint. (Part 2 of 2). For these reasons, the Court strikes from the complaint the prayer for punitive damages, together with the allegation of malice and the sentence, "Said defendant has a history of despicably ignoring rules of the road resulting in multiple moving violations, demonstrating a conscious disregard for the rights and safety of others as occurred in the present instance when she ignored a stop sign and injured plaintiff."
